Federal Reserve Watcher · Rate Decision | Date: March 16/17, 2026

Federal Reserve Watcher · Rate Decision | Date: March 16/17, 2026

Probabilistic assessment of the next Federal Reserve policy decision

As of 2026-02-07

Final Decision Engine | Meeting: March 16/17, 2026

Inputs Used: Macro Signal Synthesiser; Fed Tone Decoder; Fed Projections Synthesiser; Beige Book Summary.

  • Macro Signal: CUT (confidence: MEDIUM) — Persistent disinflationary pressures and a gradually softening labor market, coupled with pockets of financial fragility and easing liquidity, suggest policy easing is warranted (37).
  • Behavioural Feasibility: HOLD-BIASED — The FOMC held rates at the January 28, 2026 meeting, indicating a pause to assess data, though significant internal dissents for a cut and strong dovish rhetoric from key governors (Waller, Bowman, Jefferson) suggest continued easing remains highly feasible (38).
  • Fed Worldview: CUT — The Fed's dominant risk narrative is explicitly asymmetric towards downside growth/labour risks, alongside a projected disinflationary path to the 2% target, despite stable near-term growth projections and elevated near-term inflation (framed as transitory) (39).
  • Real-Economy Confirmation: MIXED — The November 2025 Beige Book confirms softening labor demand and modest wage growth, aligning with dovish arguments, and describes overall economic activity as "largely unchanged" or "modestly declining," which is slightly weaker than the Fed's SEP growth outlook, reinforcing downside risks. It also notes persistent, but contained, inflation pressures from tariffs, which the Fed views as temporary (40).

Divergence (this is signal):

  • Macro says: CUT
  • Behaviour says: HOLD
  • Fed worldview says: CUT

Decision Probabilities (sum = 100%):

  • CUT: 60%
  • HOLD: 40%
  • HIKE: 0%

Forward Guidance Bias: DOVISH — The Fed's explicit asymmetric focus on rising downside risks to employment, combined with a persistent framing of elevated inflation as transitory (due to tariffs), indicates a strong bias towards future easing and proactive support for the labor market (39)(38). Mechanism: Language shift emphasizing labor market fragility, framing inflation drivers as temporary, and readiness to adjust policy as needed.

Surprise Risk: MEDIUM — Source: Significant internal committee divergence, as evidenced by dissenting votes for a cut at the recent HOLD meeting and strong dovish calls from individual governors post-meeting. Additionally, there is data uncertainty regarding the precise trajectory of inflation and the pace of labor market softening (38).

Final Call: Outcome: CUT with dovish guidance.

The information presented is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute financial or investment advice. It has been prepared without regard to individual objectives, financial situation, or needs. You should consider whether it is appropriate for your circumstances and seek independent advice where necessary.
